title: Worsening cough and breathlessness in a smoker with new ankle swelling

chart.oneLiner: 64F with COPD, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a recent medication change presents with 4 days of worsening dyspnea, increased cough with purulent sputum, and new ankle swelling — raising COPD exacerbation versus decompensated heart failure.

opening: My breathing's been getting worse all week, I'm coughing up a lot more gunk, and now my ankles have started puffing up too.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 64-year-old woman, pursed-lip breathing, sitting forward, speaking in short three-to-four word phrases, audibly wheezy. Cooperative but tired and a little frightened by the ankle swelling.

HPI: Over the last 4 days her dyspnea has crept well above her usual baseline, with more coughing and a change from clear to thick yellow-green sputum, and louder wheeze. She had a head cold a week ago. She has been using her rescue inhaler far more often with less and less relief, and she ran out of her maintenance inhaler about ten days ago and has not refilled it. In the last few days both ankles have started to swell, which is what frightened her into coming. She CAN lie flat without getting more short of breath and does NOT wake at night gasping (no orthopnea, no PND). NO chest pain, NO palpitations she's aware of, NO fever or shaking chills beyond feeling warm, NO hemoptysis, NO calf pain, NO recent long travel or immobilization, NO weight gain noticed on her scale.
BASELINE: known COPD, chronic morning cough, gets short of breath walking one block; last spirometry showed moderate-to-severe obstruction. One hospital admission for COPD last winter, never intubated, never on home oxygen.
NEW-MEDICATION HISTORY (reveal only when asked about med changes/new pills/swelling): about three weeks ago her primary doctor added AMLODIPINE for blood pressure and switched her water pill — and her ankles have been puffier since the amlodipine started. She also finished a short course of prednisone from an urgent-care visit four days ago for the 'chest cold.'
PMH: COPD (moderate-to-severe), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, obesity, GERD, mild diabetic neuropathy. NO known heart failure, NO prior heart attack, NO atrial fibrillation that she knows of.
MEDS (reveal the list when asked what she takes): tiotropium (maintenance — OUT for ~10 days), albuterol inhaler PRN (using constantly), amlodipine (new, ~3 weeks), hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, metformin, gabapentin for neuropathy, omeprazole, and over-the-counter ibuprofen a few times a week for knees. She is usually adherent except for the lapsed tiotropium.
ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: current smoker, ~45 pack-years, still about half a pack a day. Lives with her spouse in a single-level home. Spouse is supportive and drives. Social drinker only. No drugs.
FAMILY: father had emphysema; mother had high blood pressure.
ROS: as above — fatigue and breathlessness prominent; no chest pain, no orthopnea/PND, no syncope, no fevers, denies polyuria/polydipsia beyond her usual.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: mild-to-moderate respiratory distress, accessory muscle use, pursed-lip breathing, barrel chest, speaking in short phrases. Alert, no asterixis.
- Vitals: BP 146/88, HR 112, RR 28, Temp 37.7°C, SpO2 86% RA.
- Lungs: diffuse expiratory WHEEZE, markedly prolonged expiratory phase, globally decreased breath sounds, hyperresonant to percussion. NO focal crackles, NO bronchial breathing, NO dullness — no focal consolidation. No clear bibasilar crackles of pulmonary edema.
- Cardiac: tachycardic and somewhat IRREGULAR, distant heart sounds, no murmur, no S3 gallop, PMI not displaced.
- Neck: JVP NOT elevated (sitting at 45 degrees).
- Extremities: MILD bilateral pitting ankle edema, soft and non-tender, skin intact, NO calf tenderness, NO cords, pulses intact and symmetric. (The edema is mild and symmetric, came on with the new amlodipine.)
- Abdomen: soft, no hepatomegaly, no hepatojugular reflux.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the lapsed maintenance inhaler, the new amlodipine, the recent prednisone, the smoking amount, or the recent cold unless asked the relevant question (medications/med changes, swelling, smoking, or recent illness).
- The absent orthopnea/PND, absent elevated JVP, absent S3, and the symmetric amlodipine-pattern edema are revealed only if those are asked/examined (they help distinguish from heart failure).
- Describe the heartbeat as 'a bit irregular and fast' only if asked about palpitations or when the pulse/heart is examined; do not name a rhythm.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 13.6 with neutrophilia (no bands), Hgb 15.2 (mild secondary erythrocytosis), Plt 271.
labs.BMP: Na 131, K 3.2, Cl 92, HCO3 31 (chronically elevated), BUN 28, Cr 1.3 (baseline 0.9 → mild AKI), glucose 244. Calculated anion gap = 131 − (92 + 31) = 8 (normal/low, NO anion-gap metabolic acidosis).
labs.ABG: pH 7.32, PaCO2 60, PaO2 54, HCO3 31 on room air. Chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation, now ACUTE-on-chronic (pH lower than chronic compensation alone would predict; expected HCO3 rise for chronic CO2 of 60 ≈ +7 over 24 = 31, matching).
labs.BNP: 210 pg/mL — mildly elevated but NON-diagnostic in this range; can rise with right-heart strain/cor pulmonale and age, does not by itself establish left heart failure.
labs.Magnesium: 1.4 mg/dL — low (hypomagnesemia, contributes to the atrial ectopy and the hypokalemia).
labs.VBG_lactate: Lactate 2.1 mmol/L — mildly elevated, attributable to heavy beta-agonist use and work of breathing; not shock.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Multifocal atrial tachycardia: irregularly irregular, rate ~110, at least three distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PR and P-P intervals, isoelectric baseline between P waves. Possible P pulmonale and low voltage. No acute ischemic ST changes.
ekg.verify: Source a tracing matching: irregular narrow-complex tachycardia with ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies and varying PR intervals (multifocal atrial tachycardia), low voltage, no acute ischemia — self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: Irregular narrow-complex tachycardia at ~110 bpm with at least three different P-wave shapes and varying PR/P-P intervals — multifocal atrial tachycardia — with low voltage and no acute ischemia.
searchTerms: multifocal atrial tachycardia ECG three P wave morphologies
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 64-year-old longtime smoker with a severe COPD flare, hypoxia, and low potassium/magnesium has a fast, irregular pulse.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (PA)
cxr.findings: Hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms, increased AP/retrosternal air and lucency, narrow cardiac silhouette. NO focal consolidation, NO Kerley B lines, NO cephalization, NO pleural effusions — no radiographic pulmonary edema or pneumonia.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC file showing emphysematous hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and a small/narrow heart, NOT a lobar infiltrate and NOT a CHF/edema pattern.
cxr.imageDescription: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms and a narrow cardiac silhouette; no consolidation, no pulmonary edema (no Kerley B lines/cephalization), no effusions.
searchTerms: chest radiograph hyperinflation flattened diaphragms emphysema
COPD chest x-ray no pulmonary edema
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 64-year-old with known emphysema and new ankle swelling — the film helps decide between a COPD flare and heart failure.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis for her worsening breathlessness (note your leading diagnosis). She has new bilateral ankle swelling, so explicitly address heart failure. Also name THREE common triggers/precipitants of your leading diagnosis that you would look for.
steps.rubric: COPD exacerbation (leading,6; accepts AECOPD/COPD flare). Acute decompensated heart failure — the main competing diagnosis given new bilateral edema and a mildly elevated BNP (CANT-MISS/competing,5). Pulmonary embolism — must-not-miss (CANT-MISS,4). Pneumonia (core,2). Acute coronary syndrome (core,2). Names leading dx w/ justification — lapsed maintenance inhaler, purulent sputum, viral prodrome, diffuse wheeze with hyperresonance (core,2). Names THREE triggers of a COPD exacerbation, e.g. viral/bacterial respiratory infection, air pollution/smoke exposure, medication non-adherence, or a concurrent illness/PE (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on heart failure and missing the COPD flare, or anchoring on COPD without addressing the edema/CHF (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: COPD exacerbation (lapsed maintenance tiotropium, viral prodrome, purulent sputum, diffuse expiratory wheeze with hyperresonance and acute-on-chronic hypercapnia). Main competing diagnosis to resolve: Acute decompensated heart failure — she has NEW bilateral ankle edema and a mildly elevated BNP, so it must be addressed, though the symmetric amlodipine-pattern edema, absent orthopnea/PND, non-elevated JVP, and no S3 argue against it. Must-not-miss: Pulmonary embolism. Also reasonable: Pneumonia; Acute coronary syndrome. THREE triggers of a COPD exacerbation to look for: (1) viral or bacterial respiratory infection, (2) medication non-adherence (her lapsed maintenance inhaler), and (3) environmental/pollutant or ongoing tobacco-smoke exposure — with PE and ACS as other precipitants to consider.

criticalActions.item: Acute decompensated heart failure (competing diagnosis given new edema)

coreActions.item: COPD exacerbation named as leading dx with justification
Names three triggers/precipitants of a COPD exacerbation

penalties.item: Anchors on one diagnosis without addressing the COPD-vs-heart-failure fork

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order to separate your leading diagnosis from heart failure and the other must-not-miss causes of her breathlessness?
steps.rubric: CXR to look for hyperinflation vs pulmonary edema/consolidation (CANT-MISS,4). ABG / VBG for acute-on-chronic hypercapnia (core,3). EKG — she is tachycardic and irregular (core,2). BNP to weigh heart failure (core,2). CBC (core,1). BMP (core,1). Troponin (core,1). Add electrolytes magnesium given the arrhythmia and diuretic/beta-agonist (bonus,1). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2); committing to empiric heart-failure diuresis before imaging/BNP (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CXR; ABG (or VBG); EKG; BNP; CBC; BMP; Troponin; and a magnesium level given the arrhythmia and her diuretic/beta-agonist use. Use the CXR and BNP plus the exam to weigh heart failure against a COPD flare before committing to diuresis.

bonusActions.item: Magnesium level given the arrhythmia and diuretic/beta-agonist use

optionalTests: Magnesium level given the arrhythmia and diuretic/beta-agonist use

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's own 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and their significance in this presentation.
steps.rubric: Findings = multifocal atrial tachycardia (irregular, rate ~110, ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PR/P-P, isoelectric baseline), with low voltage / possible P pulmonale and NO acute ischemia. CREDIT: identifies the rhythm as multifocal atrial tachycardia / recognizes ≥3 P-wave morphologies rather than calling it atrial fibrillation (6); recognizes NO acute ischemic ST changes (4); ties MAT to the COPD flare with hypoxia and low K/Mg as the driver, treated by treating the lungs not by cardioverting (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: mislabeling it atrial fibrillation and anticoagulating/rate-controlling as AF (-3); over-calling acute ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: multifocal atrial tachycardia with at least three P-wave morphologies
not atrial fibrillation — discrete P waves with isoelectric baseline
criticalFindings: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (irregular, ~110 bpm, ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PR and P-P intervals) with low voltage / possible P pulmonale and no acute ischemic ST changes.
commonMistakes: calling multifocal atrial tachycardia atrial fibrillation
cardioverting or anticoagulating MAT as if it were AF
steps.idealAnswer: Multifocal atrial tachycardia: an irregularly irregular narrow-complex tachycardia at ~110 bpm with at least three distinct P-wave morphologies and varying PR and P-P intervals, with an isoelectric baseline between P waves — which distinguishes it from atrial fibrillation. There is low voltage with possible P pulmonale and NO acute ischemic ST changes. MAT is essentially a marker of the severe COPD exacerbation, hypoxia, and the low potassium and magnesium; the treatment is to treat the underlying pulmonary process and correct electrolytes, not to cardiovert or anticoagulate it as AF.

criticalActions: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (irregular, ~110 bpm, ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PR and P-P intervals) with low voltage / possible P pulmonale and no acute ischemic ST changes.
coreActions: multifocal atrial tachycardia with at least three P-wave morphologies
not atrial fibrillation — discrete P waves with isoelectric baseline

penalties.label: mislabels multifocal atrial tachycardia as atrial fibrillation

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings, and say how the film helps you choose between a COPD flare and heart failure.
steps.rubric: Findings = hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter/lucency, narrow cardiac silhouette, and NO pulmonary edema (no Kerley B lines/cephalization/effusions) and NO focal consolidation. CREDIT: hyperinflation/flattened diaphragms (4); narrow heart with increased lucency/AP diameter (2); correctly states NO pulmonary edema or consolidation and uses that to argue AGAINST heart failure and pneumonia (4). PENALTY: calling a pneumonia or CHF that isn't there (-2).
keyFindings: narrow cardiac silhouette and increased lucency/AP diameter
no pulmonary edema (no Kerley B lines or cephalization) and no consolidation — argues against heart failure and pneumonia
criticalFindings: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter, and a narrow cardiac silhouette, with no pulmonary edema and no consolidation — supporting a COPD flare over heart failure or pneumonia.
commonMistakes: calling a pneumonia or CHF that isn't there
missing the absence of edema as the deliberate pertinent negative
steps.idealAnswer: Hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms, increased AP/retrosternal lucency, and a narrow cardiac silhouette — the emphysema pattern. Importantly there is NO pulmonary edema (no Kerley B lines, no vascular cephalization, no effusions) and NO focal consolidation. The clear lack of an edema pattern, together with the small heart, argues against acute heart failure as the driver of her dyspnea and against pneumonia, supporting a COPD exacerbation.

criticalActions: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter, and a narrow cardiac silhouette, with no pulmonary edema and no consolidation — supporting a COPD flare over heart failure or pneumonia.
coreActions: no pulmonary edema or consolidation — argues against heart failure and pneumonia

penalties.label: calls a pneumonia or CHF that isn't there

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Pulling the data together, what is your leading diagnosis and reasoning? Reconcile the new edema and the mildly elevated BNP, and name THREE contributors to her low sodium and low potassium/magnesium.
steps.rubric: Commits to COPD exacerbation as the driver of the dyspnea, supported by the hyperinflated edema-free film, acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis on ABG, and the multifocal atrial tachycardia as a marker of the severe flare (5). Reconciles the competing heart-failure picture — the new edema is symmetric and tracks the NEW amlodipine, with absent orthopnea/PND, non-elevated JVP, no S3, a narrow heart and no radiographic edema, and a BNP only mildly elevated (non-diagnostic and explainable by right-heart strain/age) (3). Names THREE contributors to the electrolyte abnormalities, e.g. thiazide (hyponatremia + hypokalemia), beta-agonist driving K+ intracellularly, prednisone, poor intake/AKI, or hypomagnesemia perpetuating hypokalemia (2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: COPD exacerbation. The dyspnea is driven by the flare — diffuse wheeze with hyperinflation and an edema-free chest film, acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis (pH 7.32 / PaCO2 60 / HCO3 31) on the ABG, and multifocal atrial tachycardia as a marker of severe COPD with hypoxia and electrolyte depletion. Reconciling the competing heart-failure picture: her new bilateral ankle edema is mild, symmetric, and began with the NEW amlodipine (a dihydropyridine that causes peripheral edema); she has NO orthopnea or PND, a non-elevated JVP, no S3, a narrow cardiac silhouette with no radiographic pulmonary edema, and a BNP that is only mildly elevated (210) — non-diagnostic in this range and explainable by right-heart strain/cor pulmonale and age. So heart failure is not the driver, though it remains on the radar. THREE contributors to her low sodium and low potassium/magnesium: (1) hydrochlorothiazide (causes hyponatremia and renal potassium/magnesium wasting), (2) heavy beta-agonist use shifting potassium intracellularly, and (3) the recent prednisone plus poor intake with a prerenal AKI — with the low magnesium itself perpetuating the refractory hypokalemia. The discriminating data are the chest film, the BNP, and the exam; if the BNP or volume picture were truly ambiguous, a bedside echocardiogram would settle left-ventricular function.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition and what is needed for a safe discharge once she has stabilized?
steps.rubric: Per MGH COPD: short-acting bronchodilators albuterol + ipratropium (CANT-MISS,5); systemic corticosteroids prednisone 40 mg ×5 d (CANT-MISS,4); antibiotics given increased dyspnea + sputum volume + purulence (4); controlled O2 targeting SpO2 88-92% (3); NIV/BiPAP for worsening hypercapnic respiratory failure/acidosis (3); correct the hypokalemia AND hypomagnesemia and treat the MAT by treating the lungs/hypoxia rather than cardioverting — avoid a non-selective beta-blocker (2); review the amlodipine as the cause of edema and restart the lapsed maintenance inhaler (bonus,2); safe-discharge plan — confirm clinical/oxygenation stability, medication reconciliation (resume tiotropium, address amlodipine edema, hold/limit NSAID), inhaler-technique teaching, smoking-cessation support, vaccinations, and close follow-up with the spouse engaged (2). PENALTY: uncontrolled high-flow O2 driving CO2 retention without monitoring (-3); reflexively diuresing/anticoagulating/cardioverting the MAT as if it were heart failure or AF (-3); discharging while still hypoxemic/hypercapnic without a plan (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: COPD exacerbation: inhaled short-acting bronchodilators (albuterol + ipratropium), systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg × 5 days), and antibiotics (e.g., azithromycin or doxycycline) given the increased dyspnea, sputum volume, and purulence. Controlled oxygen titrated to SpO2 88–92% — avoid uncontrolled high-flow O2 that worsens hypercapnia — with NIV/BiPAP if she tires or her hypercapnic acidosis worsens. Replace potassium AND magnesium; the multifocal atrial tachycardia is treated by treating the underlying flare, hypoxia, and electrolytes (NOT by cardioversion or anticoagulation), and avoid a non-selective beta-blocker that could worsen bronchospasm. Do NOT reflexively diurese — the edema is from the new amlodipine, which should be reviewed/changed, and the lapsed tiotropium should be resumed; hold/limit the OTC ibuprofen. Disposition: admit (telemetry given the MAT and electrolytes; ICU/step-down if she needs NIV). Safe discharge once stabilized requires: confirmed clinical and oxygenation stability off escalating support, full medication reconciliation (resume maintenance inhaler, address the amlodipine-related edema, reconcile diuretic/NSAID), inhaler-technique teaching, smoking-cessation support and pharmacotherapy, influenza/pneumococcal/COVID vaccination, a written action plan, and close PCP/pulmonary follow-up with her spouse engaged for transport and support.

coreActions.item: NIV (BiPAP) if hypercapnic respiratory failure worsens
Correct K+ and Mg and treat MAT by treating the flare, not cardioversion; avoid non-selective beta-blocker
Safe-discharge plan: stability check, med reconciliation, inhaler teaching, smoking cessation, vaccines, close follow-up

bonusActions.item: Review amlodipine as cause of edema and resume lapsed maintenance inhaler

penalties.item: Reflexively diuresing/anticoagulating/cardioverting as heart failure or AF

optionalActions: NIV (BiPAP) if hypercapnic respiratory failure worsens
Correct K+ and Mg and treat MAT by treating the flare, not cardioversion; avoid non-selective beta-blocker
Safe-discharge plan: stability check, med reconciliation, inhaler teaching, smoking cessation, vaccines, close follow-up
Review amlodipine as cause of edema and resume lapsed maintenance inhaler
unsafeActions: Reflexively diuresing/anticoagulating/cardioverting as heart failure or AF
steps.disposition: Admit with telemetry; ICU/step-down if NIV or worsening hypercapnic failure

physicalExamMappings.finding: Lungs: diffuse expiratory WHEEZE, markedly prolonged expiratory phase, globally decreased breath sounds, hyperresonant to percussion. NO focal crackles or consolidation; no clear bibasilar crackles of pulmonary edema.
Neck: JVP NOT elevated at 45 degrees (argues against heart failure as the driver).
Cardiac: tachycardic and somewhat IRREGULAR, distant heart sounds, no murmur, NO S3 gallop, PMI not displaced.
Extremities: MILD bilateral pitting ankle edema, soft and non-tender, symmetric, skin intact (came on with the new amlodipine).
Extremities: NO calf tenderness, no cords, pulses intact and symmetric (argues against DVT).
General: mild-to-moderate respiratory distress, accessory muscle use, pursed-lip breathing, barrel chest, speaking in short phrases. Alert, no asterixis.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 64F with COPD, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, obesity and a recent medication change presents with 4 days of worsening dyspnea, purulent sputum, and NEW bilateral ankle edema, creating a genuine COPD-exacerbation-versus-heart-failure fork. The diagnosis is COPD exacerbation: an edema-free hyperinflated chest film with a narrow heart, acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis on ABG, and multifocal atrial tachycardia on the EKG, while the new symmetric edema tracks the newly started amlodipine and the only-mildly-elevated BNP is non-diagnostic. Multi-system labs (hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, mild AKI, hyperglycemia) reflect her thiazide, heavy beta-agonist use, recent prednisone, and poor intake. It is reached by eliciting the focused and medication history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while resolving the heart-failure fork and excluding PE/pneumonia/ACS.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Lungs: diffuse expiratory WHEEZE, markedly prolonged expiratory phase, globally decreased breath sounds, hyperresonant to percussion. NO focal crackles or consolidation; no clear bibasilar crackles of pulmonary edema.
Neck: JVP NOT elevated at 45 degrees (argues against heart failure as the driver).
Cardiac: tachycardic and somewhat IRREGULAR, distant heart sounds, no murmur, NO S3 gallop, PMI not displaced.
Extremities: MILD bilateral pitting ankle edema, soft and non-tender, symmetric, skin intact (came on with the new amlodipine).
Extremities: NO calf tenderness, no cords, pulses intact and symmetric (argues against DVT).
General: mild-to-moderate respiratory distress, accessory muscle use, pursed-lip breathing, barrel chest, speaking in short phrases. Alert, no asterixis.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the new edema that raises heart failure.
Elicit the focused history including the medication history (lapsed maintenance inhaler, new amlodipine, recent prednisone) and the orthopnea/PND screen.
Perform the targeted physical exam to reveal wheeze with hyperresonance, non-elevated JVP, no S3, and symmetric soft ankle edema.
Build a differential that explicitly forks COPD exacerbation against acute decompensated heart failure while keeping PE, pneumonia, and ACS in view.
Order a focused workup and interpret the integrated EKG (multifocal atrial tachycardia) and CXR (hyperinflation without edema) plus the multi-system labs.
Commit to COPD exacerbation, reconcile the edema/BNP and electrolytes, and manage with bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics, controlled O2/NIV, electrolyte repletion, and a safe-discharge plan.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on heart failure and diuresing amlodipine-related edema
Mislabeling multifocal atrial tachycardia as atrial fibrillation and anticoagulating/cardioverting
Uncontrolled high-flow O2 driving CO2 retention
Missing the lapsed maintenance inhaler as the precipitant
managementPearls: Treat MAT by treating the COPD flare, hypoxia, and electrolytes — not by cardioversion
criticalInterpretationItems: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (irregular, ~110 bpm, ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PR and P-P intervals) with low voltage / possible P pulmonale and no acute ischemic ST changes.

Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter, and a narrow cardiac silhouette, with no pulmonary edema and no consolidation — supporting a COPD flare over heart failure or pneumonia.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (irregular, ~110 bpm, ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PR and P-P intervals) with low voltage / possible P pulmonale and no acute ischemic ST changes.

Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter, and a narrow cardiac silhouette, with no pulmonary edema and no consolidation — supporting a COPD flare over heart failure or pneumonia.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (irregular, ~110 bpm, ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PR and P-P intervals) with low voltage / possible P pulmonale and no acute ischemic ST changes.

Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter, and a narrow cardiac silhouette, with no pulmonary edema and no consolidation — supporting a COPD flare over heart failure or pneumonia.
imageInterpretationPearls: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (≥3 P-wave morphologies, varying PR) is a marker of severe pulmonary disease — treat the lungs and electrolytes, do not treat it as atrial fibrillation.
On a dyspnea film with new edema, the deliberate pertinent negative is the ABSENCE of pulmonary edema and the narrow heart — it argues against heart failure.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern about her swelling and breathing
Asks non-judgmentally about smoking and inhaler adherence
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
